overview.body: This is the capstone. You have built the same road trip game five times across this semester, once in each module, each time with better tools. This week you finish the transformation. Two things happen: First, you extend city.py with WinCity and DeadEndCity subclasses that override get_arrival_message(), so each city type announces itself differently. Second, you complete game.py with the full game loop: trivia gate, travel, hazards, and the final report. Then you write main.py, which is now four lines. The game is the same. The architecture is unrecognizable from where you started.

mentorQuote.quote: The player has no idea you refactored. They still type a city name and roll a D20. But look at main.py: four lines. The whole game is four lines. That is what good architecture feels like from the outside. Invisible.

objectives: Define WinCity and DeadEndCity as subclasses of City using inheritance
Call the superclass constructor from a subclass using City.__init__(self, ...)
Override get_arrival_message() in each subclass for type-specific behavior
Complete the Game class with play_turn(), trivia gate, hazard handling, and final report
Write main.py that runs the entire game in four lines
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 11: Programs 11-1 and 11-2 (vehicles.py, superclass with subclasses, calling superclass constructor) and Program 11-9 (animals.py, method overriding). Chapter 10: Game class completes the pattern from Program 10-9.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapters 10 and 11
programRequirements: city.py contains City base class plus WinCity and DeadEndCity subclasses
WinCity and DeadEndCity call City.__init__(self, ...) and override get_arrival_message()
main.py creates a Game, prints a title, runs the game loop, and displays the final report
All files have documentation headers and all methods have docstrings
overview.additionalFilesNote: Submit city.py, player.py, game.py, main.py, and function_library.py. The full game must run from main.py without errors.
overview.filenameNote: Submit main.py, city.py, player.py, game.py, and function_library.py this week.

rows.task: WinCity and DeadEndCity in city.py: inheritance, constructor calls, method overrides
play_turn(), trivia gate, travel loop, hazard handler, final report

rows.checkpoint: CP3: Write main.py and Full QA
rows.task: Four-line main.py, full playthrough, edge cases, CodeGrade

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is the complete final architecture. The [NEW] tags show what you are adding this week. The [REPLACED] tags show what changes from 5.2. Everything else carries forward.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- city.py [EXTENDED] ----
class City:                     (from 5.1, add get_arrival_message)
│   get_arrival_message()       [NEW] base version: 'Welcome to Name'
│
class WinCity(City):            [NEW] subclass for beach cities
│   __init__(name, tagline)     calls City.__init__(self,name,'WIN',tagline)
│   get_arrival_message()       [NEW-override] beach winner message
│
class DeadEndCity(City):        [NEW] subclass for dead ends
│   __init__(name, tagline, warning)
│   get_arrival_message()       [NEW-override] warning message

# ---- player.py (UNCHANGED) ----
# ---- function_library.py (UNCHANGED) ----

# ---- game.py [COMPLETED] ----
class Game:
│── __init__()              (uses WinCity/DeadEndCity now)
│── __setup_cities()        [REPLACED] creates typed subclass objects
│── start_new_game()        (unchanged)
│── get_current_city()      (unchanged)
│── get_player()            (unchanged)
│── play_turn()             [NEW] one full turn of the game loop
│── __trivia_gate()         [NEW] private: ask trivia, return destination
│── __travel()              [NEW] private: D20 loop to destination
│── __handle_hazard()       [NEW] private: random hazard from dict
│── is_game_over()          [NEW] returns self.__game_over
│── did_win()               [NEW] returns self.__won
│── display_final_report()  [NEW] prints full trip summary

# ---- main.py [NEW] ----
from game import Game
def main():
    game = Game()
    game.start_new_game()
    while not game.is_game_over():
        game.play_turn()
    game.display_final_report()
if __name__ == '__main__': main()

checkpoints.title: Add WinCity and DeadEndCity to city.py

content.body: Open city.py. First, add a get_arrival_message() method to the base City class. This is a default version that returns a generic welcome. Then add two subclasses below it: WinCity for beach destinations and DeadEndCity for dead ends. Both inherit from City and override get_arrival_message() with their own version.

items: In the base City class, add get_arrival_message(self) at the end: return f'Welcome to {self.get_name()}! "{self.get_tagline()}"'. Use getter methods rather than self.__name directly, because subclasses cannot access the superclass's private attributes.

After the closing of the City class, define class WinCity(City):. Write its __init__(self, name, tagline): the first line must be City.__init__(self, name, 'WIN', tagline) to initialize all the inherited attributes. Add any WinCity-specific attributes after that if desired.
Override get_arrival_message(self) in WinCity: return a multi-line string announcing that the player has won and reached the beach. Include the city name and tagline.
Define class DeadEndCity(City):. Its __init__(self, name, tagline, warning) calls City.__init__(self, name, 'DEAD_END', tagline) and stores self.__warning = warning as a private attribute.
Override get_arrival_message(self) in DeadEndCity: return a string that includes the city name and the warning message.

Open a Python shell and test: create a WinCity('Corpus Christi', 'Sparkling City') and a DeadEndCity('Laredo', 'Gateway', 'No beach this way!'). Call is_win_city(), is_dead_end(), and get_arrival_message() on each. Verify the right message appears for each type.

references.description: Dog.__init__ calls Mammal.__init__(self, 'Dog'). This is exactly the pattern WinCity uses. City.__init__(self, name, 'WIN', tagline) initializes all the inherited private attributes that WinCity cannot set directly.

Car inherits from Automobile and adds its own attribute (doors) after calling the superclass constructor. DeadEndCity does the same: it inherits from City and adds __warning after calling City.__init__.

content.title: Use Getters in get_arrival_message()
content.body: In the base City class, get_arrival_message() can use self.__name directly. But in WinCity and DeadEndCity, you cannot use self.__name. That attribute belongs to City and is name-mangled to _City__name. Use self.get_name() and self.get_tagline() in all three override versions to be safe.

content.title: CP1 Complete
items: City base class has get_arrival_message() returning a generic welcome
WinCity(City) calls City.__init__(self, name, 'WIN', tagline) and overrides get_arrival_message()
DeadEndCity(City) calls City.__init__(self, name, 'DEAD_END', tagline) and stores __warning
DeadEndCity overrides get_arrival_message() to include the warning
Manual test confirms is_win_city() and is_dead_end() return correct values for each subclass
Manual test confirms each subclass returns its own arrival message

content.body: Open game.py. Add two state attributes to __init__, update __setup_cities() to use the new subclasses, then add the remaining methods that complete the game loop. This is the biggest checkpoint of the week. Work through it in the order listed.

items: Update the imports in game.py: add from city import City, WinCity, DeadEndCity.

In __init__, add two new state attributes after the existing ones: self.__game_over = False and self.__won = False. Also add self.__hazards = {} and call self.__setup_hazards() after self.__setup_cities().

Update __setup_cities(): change corpus = City('Corpus Christi', 'WIN', ...) to corpus = WinCity('Corpus Christi', ...). Change Laredo and El Paso to DeadEndCity objects. The hub cities (Houston, Austin, Dallas) remain plain City objects.
Write __setup_hazards(self): populate self.__hazards with the same hazard dictionary from your Module 4 game (Buc-ee's, engine light, armadillo, speed trap, etc.).
Write is_game_over(self): return self.__game_over. Write did_win(self): return self.__won.

Write play_turn(self): get the current City object, display it, show destinations, get the player's numeric choice using function_library.get_valid_int(), call self.__trivia_gate() with the chosen destination, call self.__travel() with the result, then call dest_city.get_arrival_message() and print it. Check dest_city.is_win_city() and dest_city.is_dead_end() to update self.__game_over and self.__won accordingly.

Write __trivia_gate(self, chosen_dest, distance, all_destinations): call dest_city.get_random_trivia(). If None, auto-pass. Otherwise present the question, get the player's answer using function_library.clean_input(), compare, record the result on the player with self.__player.record_trivia(correct), and return (final_destination, final_distance).

Write __travel(self, destination, distance): run the D20 loop using function_library.roll_d20(), call self.__player.add_turn() and self.__player.add_miles() each iteration, check for hazards with function_library.check_percentage(30), and when the remaining distance hits zero call self.__player.travel_to(destination, distance).
Write __handle_hazard(self): pick a random hazard key, flip a coin with function_library.flip_coin(), print the result, and call self.__player.record_hazard(hazard_name, escaped).
Write display_final_report(self): print the player's name, route string, unique cities (sorted), trivia stats with accuracy percentage, hazard stats, total miles and turns, and win/loss status.

content.title: Polymorphism in play_turn()
content.body: When you call dest_city.get_arrival_message() in play_turn(), Python automatically calls the right version. If dest_city is a WinCity, it calls WinCity.get_arrival_message(). If it is a plain City, it calls City.get_arrival_message(). The Game class does not need to check the type. The objects handle it themselves.

content.quote: I tried to reach a dead end city. play_turn() crashed. The game_over flag never got set. I tried to go to a win city. Same issue. Then I found a city with no trivia. auto-pass was not implemented. I found six bugs in ten minutes. Test every branch.

content.title: CP2 Complete
items: game.py imports WinCity and DeadEndCity from city
__setup_cities() creates WinCity and DeadEndCity objects for the appropriate cities
play_turn() runs one full turn including trivia, travel, hazards, and arrival check
is_game_over() returns True after reaching a win city or dead end with no return
display_final_report() prints route, unique cities, trivia accuracy, hazard stats, and miles

content.body: Create main.py. Then play your game from start to finish before submitting.

items: Create main.py with a documentation header. Import from game import Game.
Write main(): create a Game object, print a title block, call game.start_new_game(), run while not game.is_game_over(): game.play_turn(), then call game.display_final_report().
Add the if __name__ == '__main__': guard and call main().
Run the game end to end. Play until you reach a beach city. Verify the WinCity arrival message appears. Verify the final report shows accurate trivia stats, unique cities, and hazard counts.
Run again and try to reach a dead end city. Verify the DeadEndCity message appears and the game handles it correctly.
Trigger at least one hazard and verify it is recorded in the final report.
Submit all five files to CodeGrade: main.py, city.py, player.py, game.py, and function_library.py.

content.title: MASTER Week: Solution Quality Review
content.body: Anjali will review your submission for Solution Quality this week. Clean variable names, consistent method naming, no unused variables, and well-structured methods all matter. If a method is longer than about 20 lines, consider whether part of it belongs in a helper method.

content.title: Lab 5.3 Complete
items: main.py runs the full game cleanly from a single call
WinCity and DeadEndCity produce distinct arrival messages
Trivia accuracy, unique cities, and hazard stats are accurate in the final report
All five files submitted to CodeGrade
Documentation headers and docstrings present in all files
Flake8 returns no warnings on any file

beforeYouSubmit: city.py contains City base class plus WinCity and DeadEndCity subclasses
WinCity and DeadEndCity call City.__init__(self, ...) as the first line of their __init__
All three versions of get_arrival_message() use getter methods (get_name(), get_tagline()) not private attributes
game.py is complete: play_turn(), trivia gate, travel loop, hazard handler, final report, is_game_over(), did_win()
main.py creates a Game and runs the game loop (fewer than 15 lines total)
All five files have documentation headers and all methods have docstrings
Game runs from start to finish without errors
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Full Chapter 10 and 11 technique set: inheritance, calling superclass constructors, method overriding, and polymorphism. All Module 1–4 techniques also permitted.

specific: Method overriding: redefining get_arrival_message() in each subclass
Polymorphism: calling city.get_arrival_message() on any City subclass
isinstance() if needed for type checking
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list comprehensions, multiple inheritance, abstract base classes

warnings: MASTER Week: Anjali will review for Solution Quality. Methods should be focused and well-named.
CodeGrade tests each class file in isolation. A syntax error in city.py will fail all city-related tests even if game.py works fine.

ifYouGetStuck: If WinCity.get_arrival_message() crashes with AttributeError, you are trying to access self.__name directly. Use self.get_name() instead
If the game loop runs forever, check that play_turn() sets self.__game_over = True when the player reaches a win or dead end city
Re-read Program 11-9 (animals.py) if the inheritance syntax is not clicking. WinCity(City) is structurally identical to Dog(Mammal)
Come to Zoom or office hours with your specific error message. This is the hardest week of the semester and the most worth getting help on
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 11 BookEx Reference Guide (Programs 11-9 and 11-1 through 11-3) and Chapter 10 BookEx Reference Guide (Programs 10-9 and 10-10)
